title-group.article-title: A study on the effect of blood flow restriction training on the recovery of lower limb motor function in stroke patients with hemiplegia: a randomized controlled trial

contrib-group.aff: 1https://ror.org/01673gn35grid.413387.a0000 0004 1758 177XRehabilitation Medicine Department, Affiliated Hospital of North Sichuan Medical College, Nanchong, Sichuan 637008 China

2https://ror.org/05n50qc07grid.452642.3Department of Gastrointestinal Hernia Surgery, The Second Clinical Medical College of North Sichuan Medical College, Nanchong Central Hospital (Beijing Anzhen Nanchong Hospital), Nanchong, Sichuan 63700 China

The images or other third party material in this article are included in the article’s Creative Commons licence, unless indicated otherwise in a credit line to the material. If material is not included in the article’s Creative Commons licence and your intended use is not permitted by statutory regulation or exceeds the permitted use, you will need to obtain permission directly from the copyright holder. To view a copy of this licence, visit http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc-nd/4.0/.

sec.title: Background
sec.p: To investigate the effect of blood flow restriction training (BFRT) on lower limb motor function recovery in stroke patients with hemiplegia.

sec.title: Methods
sec.p: In this parallel-group, assessor-blinded, randomized controlled trial, 80 patients with first-ever stroke (ischemic or hemorrhagic) were enrolled. Participants were randomly allocated to receive either routine rehabilitation (RR group, n = 40) or routine rehabilitation plus BFRT (RR + BFRT group, n = 40) for 8 weeks (5 days/week). Assessments performed before and after the intervention included the Fugl-Meyer Assessment of the Lower Extremity (FMA-LE), Berg Balance Scale (BBS), Modified Barthel Index (MBI), 6-Minute Walk Test (6MWT), and Manual Muscle Testing (MMT) for key lower limb muscles.

sec.title: Results
sec.p: Baseline characteristics and all outcome measures were comparable between the two groups (all P > 0.05). After the intervention, both groups showed significant within-group improvements in FMA-LE, BBS, MBI, 6MWT, and MMT scores (all P < 0.05). The RR + BFRT group demonstrated significantly greater improvement than the RR group in FMA-LE, BBS, MBI, 6MWT, and MMT scores for the iliopsoas, quadriceps, and hamstrings (all P < 0.05). No between-group difference was found for tibialis anterior muscle strength (P > 0.05).

sec.title: Conclusions
sec.p: Adjunctive BFRT can effectively enhance lower limb motor function, balance, walking capacity, and muscle strength in stroke patients with hemiplegia, leading to greater functional independence. BFRT appears to be a beneficial adjunct to routine stroke rehabilitation.

sec.title: Supplementary Information
sec.p: The online version contains supplementary material available at 10.1186/s13063-026-09528-3.

funding-source.institution: Development Plan Project of Affiliated Hospital of North Sichuan Medical College

custom-meta.meta-value: © BioMed Central Ltd., part of Springer Nature 2026

sec.title: Introduction
sec.p: Stroke is the most common cause of disability, the second leading cause of dementia, and the fourth leading cause of death in the developed world, with an annual incidence of 150–200 per 100,000 individuals. Approximately one in seven people will experience a stroke in their lifetime [1]. Limb hemiplegia is a predominant clinical manifestation of stroke, yet conventional rehabilitation often yields limited restorative effects. In the acute phase, up to 80% of stroke patients exhibit limb hypokinesia, and even with rehabilitation, only 5% to 20% achieve full functional recovery of the affected extremities [2, 3].

Post-stroke motor dysfunction, encompassed by weakness, dystonia, and abnormal gait, constitutes a primary disability. This dysfunction can lead to secondary complications such as decline in cardiopulmonary function, increased risk of thrombosis, and muscle atrophy, thereby significantly reducing patients’ capacity for activities of daily living and overall quality of life [4]. Standard rehabilitation strategies for addressing these deficits include muscle strength training, neurodevelopmental treatment (NDT), motor relearning programs (MRPs), constraint-induced movement therapy (CIMT), and neuromuscular electrical stimulation (NMES), among others [5–7].

BFRT has gained increasing recognition in both scientific research and public health for its significant role in rehabilitation [8]. Originally developed by Dr. Y. Sato in Japan in 1967, the technique involves applying external pressure (typically 50–200 mmHg) to the proximal limbs using tourniquets, inflatable cuffs, or elastic bandages [9]. This pressure partially restricts venous return while maintaining arterial inflow. When combined with low-load resistance training (at 20–30% of one-repetition maximum), BFRT promotes muscle hypertrophy, enhances muscular adaptation, and improves strength. Proposed underlying mechanisms include the enhanced recruitment of fast-twitch muscle fibers under hypoxic conditions, the moderate accumulation of reactive oxygen species that may stimulate anabolic pathways, and increased secretion of catecholamines and growth hormones [10–12]. As an intervention that modulates arterial inflow and venous outflow, BFRT is now widely applied in rehabilitation and sports medicine, spurring extensive research into its physiological effects, practical applications, and safety profile [13].

A key advantage of BFRT is its ability to elicit training benefits comparable to high-intensity exercise while using substantially lower mechanical loads. This innovative approach effectively stimulates muscle growth and enhances neuromuscular adaptability, thereby promoting the recovery of limb motor function [14, 15]. Evidence suggests that BFRT combined with conventional rehabilitation can improve upper limb muscle strength in patients with incomplete spinal cord injury [16]. Furthermore, studies indicate that adjunctive BFRT can more effectively promote the recovery of knee joint function—particularly in quadriceps strength and thickness—following arthroscopic partial meniscectomy [17]. However, whether BFRT can enhance lower limb muscle strength and functional outcomes in patients with post-stroke hemiplegia remains unreported in the literature. Therefore, this study aimed to investigate the effect of BFRT combined with routine rehabilitation on lower limb function in hemiplegic stroke patients, thereby providing evidence for a novel rehabilitation strategy.

sec.title: Study design and participants
sec.p: This study was approved by the Ethical Committee of the Affiliated Hospital of North Sichuan Medical College (2023ER378-1) and registered with the Chinese Clinical Research Registration Center (ChiCTR2500095271). All methods were performed in accordance with relevant guidelines and regulations. This was a parallel-group, two-arm, assessor-blinded, randomized controlled trial employing a between-subject repeated-measures design, reported according to the CONSORT 2025 statement.

Eighty-two stroke patients (including both ischemic and hemorrhagic subtypes) who met the inclusion criteria were recruited from the Rehabilitation Medical Center between July 2020 and December 2022. After randomization, one participant from each group dropped out. Ultimately, 80 participants completed the study and were analyzed: the routine rehabilitation group (RR group, n = 40) and the routine rehabilitation plus BFRT group (RR + BFRT group, n = 40) (Fig. 1). All participants provided written informed consent. Baseline demographic and clinical characteristics were well-balanced between groups (all P > 0.05, Table 1). Table 1
General demographic data of the patients
VariableRRn = 40RR + BFRTn = 40t/x2P
Sex (male/female)30/1032/80.2780.592
Age(years)58.90 ± 7.77557.60 ± 7.9800.5450.463
BMI (kg/m2)25.8 ± 3.024.8 ± 3.02.2470.138
DD(days)45.7 ± 14.645.9 ± 13.90.0050.944
Smoking, n (%)22(55.0%)25 (62.5%)0.4640.496
Comorbidities, n (%)
Hypertension33 (82.5%)27 (67.5%)2.4000.121
Diabetes20 (50.0%)17 (42.5%)0.4530.501
Cardiovascular11 (27.5%)19 (47.5%)3.4130.065
Stroke type, n (%)
Ischemic12 (30.0%)18 (45.0%)1.9200.166
Hemorrhagic28 (70.0%)22 (55.0%)
Hemiplegic side, n (%)
Right19 (47.5%)16 (40.0%)0.4570.499
Left21 (52.5%)24 (60.0%)
MMSE Score21.88 ± 3.7020.90 ± 4.101.1170.267
BMI body mass index, DD disease duration, RR routine rehabilitation group, BFRT blood flow restriction training group, MMSE mini-mental state examinationFig. 1
Flowchart overview of the study

sec.title: Inclusion criteria
sec.p: Patients were included if they (1) met the Chinese diagnostic criteria for cerebrovascular diseases (2019 edition); (2) had confirmed hemorrhagic or ischemic lesions via CT or MRI; (3) were experiencing their first stroke or a recurrent stroke without significant residual impairment from prior events; (4) had stable vital signs (SBP 110–180 mmHg, DBP 60–110 mmHg, HR 50–100 bpm, SpO2 ≥ 95% on room air) for 24 h pre-enrollment, were conscious, had no significant cognitive impairment (MMSE score > 17), and could participate voluntarily and cooperatively; and (5) provided signed informed consent.

sec.title: Exclusion criteria
sec.p: Individuals were excluded if they (1) had cognitive dysfunction precluding consent or participation; (2) had severe organ failure (e.g., heart, liver, kidney); (3) were deaf-mute, had comprehension disorders, or had a history of mental illness or tumors; (4) had cerebellar or vestibular impairments causing balance dysfunction; (5) had skin injuries on the treated lower limb; (6) had a recent history of lower limb fractures (within 6 months) or any current fracture affecting weight-bearing; (7) had a diagnosis of peripheral vascular disease (PVD), defined as an ankle-brachial index < 0.9, a history of intermittent claudication, or prior intervention for PVD; and (8) were unable to complete the training as required.

All participants completed the planned 8-week intervention with high adherence (> 90% of sessions attended in both groups). No serious adverse events occurred. Through systematic monitoring, no instances of severe numbness, delayed-onset muscle soreness beyond typical levels, blood pressure fluctuations requiring intervention, or symptoms suggestive of deep vein thrombosis were reported in either group. One participant in the RR group was withdrawn following a fatal non-treatment-related complication, and one in the RR + BFRT group was lost to follow-up.

sec.title: Sample size calculation
sec.p: The sample size was calculated a priori using G*Power 3.1 software. With the F model selected to achieve 95% power at α = 0.05, two groups, and four measurement repetitions, the primary endpoint was the change in the Lower-Extremity Fugl-Meyer Assessment (FMA-LE) score after 8 weeks. Based on a previous pilot study and similar literature [1], the calculation yielded a required sample size of n = 80. Accounting for a potential 20% attrition rate, the target enrollment was set at 100 participants. The final analyzed sample comprised 40 subjects per group.

sec.title: Randomization and blinding
sec.p: Randomization and allocation concealment were managed by an independent research assistant not involved in recruitment or assessment. A computer-generated random number sequence (using www.randomizer.org) was created in a 1:1 ratio, stratified by stroke type (ischemic/hemorrhagic) [18]. Each allocation was sealed in sequentially numbered, opaque envelopes. After obtaining baseline assessments and informed consent, the treating physiotherapist (who was not involved in outcome assessment) opened the next envelope in sequence to reveal the group assignment. Outcome assessors were fully blinded to group allocation throughout the study. Assessments were conducted in a separate room, and participants were instructed not to discuss details of their training with the assessor. All outcome assessments were performed by two trained physiotherapists who were not involved in delivering the interventions and were kept blinded to group allocation throughout the trial.

sec.title: Routine rehabilitation
sec.p:
All patients in both the RR group and the RR + BFRT group received a standardized, multi-modal conventional rehabilitation program five days per week for eight weeks. The program was designed based on current clinical guidelines for stroke rehabilitation to comprehensively address motor impairment, postural control, and functional mobility. The daily session had a total duration of 60 minutes and was composed of the following components, delivered in a fixed order by licensed physiotherapists [19–22] (Table 2). The content, duration, and intensity of this conventional rehabilitation core were strictly equivalent between the two groups; the sole difference was the addition of the 20-minute BFRT protocol in the RR + BFRT group. Table 2
Standardized conventional rehabilitation program (daily session)
ComponentContent and techniqueDuration (daily)Rationale
Proprioceptive Neuromuscular Facilitation (PNF)Techniques including rhythmic initiation,hold-relax,and combinateion of isotonics were applied to lower limb D1 and D2 diagonal patterns15 minutesTo facilitate motor unit recruitment, enhance muscular coordination, regulate tone, and improve limb awareness through proprioceptive stimulation
Bobath/Neurodevelopmental Treatment (NDT)Focused on key points of control to facilitate normal movement patterns. Activities included weight-shifting in sitting and standing, balance reaction training, and task-oriented practice to inhibit abnormal synergies15 minutesTo improve postural control, promote selective movement, and translate gains into functional tasks by re-educating normal movement
Neuromuscular Electrical Stimulation (NMES)Applied to four key muscle groups on the paretic side: iliopsoas, quadriceps, hamstrings, and tibialis anterior. Electrodes (50 × 90 mm for hip/thigh, 50 × 50 mm for ankle) were placed over muscle bellies. Parameters: frequency 35 Hz, pulse width 250 µs, on/off cycle of 10s/20s, with intensity titrated to produce a visible muscle contraction without discomfort (typically 8–18 mA)15 minutesTo provide neuromuscular activation, combat disuse atrophy, and maintain muscle metabolic capacity during the early recovery phase
Functional and Task-Specific TrainingThis integrated component included transfer training (e.g., sit-to-stand), over-ground walking practice with appropriate assistive devices, step negotiation, and activities of daily living (ADL) rehearsal (e.g., toileting transfers)15 minutesTo directly practice and consolidate functional skills essential for independence, ensuring rehabilitation gains translate into real-world abilities
AcupunctureStandardized acupuncture was administered by a certified practitioner at selected points (e.g., Zusanli ST36, Yanglingquan GB34, Sanyinjiao SP6) on the affected lower limb, aiming to regulate qi and blood flow according to traditional Chinese medicine principlesIntegrated within the session timeUsed as a complementary modality in our clinical setting, based on its reported benefits in promoting muscle recovery and reducing spasticity post-stroke
Total Duration (Both Groups)60 minutes
Additional Intervention (RR + BFRT Group only)Blood Flow Restriction Training (BFRT) as described in Sect. "Blood flow restriction training (BFRT)" + 20 minutes

sec.title: Blood flow restriction training (BFRT)
sec.p:
Participants in the RR + BFRT group received BFRT combined with active exercise. A pneumatic tourniquet cuff (7 cm width) was placed on the proximal thigh of the affected limb. The pressure was set at 160–170 mmHg. The training session consisted of two cycles: an initial 10-minute exercise period, a 1-minute pressure release, and a second 20-minute exercise period. During both pressurized cycles, participants performed a structured, low-intensity routine including seated ankle dorsiflexion/plantarflexion, straight leg raises, and seated knee extension/flexion. Exercises were performed at a perceived intensity of “light effort” (approximately 20–30% of estimated 1-repetition maximum), with 15 repetitions per set and 30-second rest intervals. The total daily BFRT session duration was 20 minutes of pressurized time, administered once daily, 5 days per week for 8 weeks (Fig. 2). Before and after each BFRT session, the treated limb was inspected for color, sensation, and active movement. Participants were explicitly questioned about symptoms of numbness, paresthesia, excessive pain (rated > 7/10 on a visual analog scale), or discomfort. Blood pressure was measured pre- and post-training. Any adverse event was to be recorded and reported immediately. Fig. 2
Schematic diagram of blood flow restriction training

Treatment adherence was high. Participants in the RR group attended a mean of 38.2 ± 2.1 out of 40 scheduled sessions (95.5% adherence), and those in the RR + BFRT group attended 37.8 ± 2.4 sessions (94.5% adherence). The difference in adherence between groups was not statistically significant (t(78) = 0.85, P = 0.40).

sec.title: Safety monitoring and adverse events
sec.p:
Adverse events (AEs) were systematically monitored and recorded. These included the following:
Local AEs related to the BFRT procedure: excessive pain (rated > 7/10 on a visual analog scale), persistent numbness or paresthesia, skin irritation or petechiae under the cuff
Systemic AEs: symptomatic hypotension or hypertension (e.g., dizziness, headache), palpitations, or any other discomfort reported by the participant
Serious AEs (SAEs): any event resulting in hospitalization, significant disability, or deemed life-threatening.

sec.title: Fugl-Meyer Assessment—Lower Extremity (FMA-LE)
sec.p: The Fugl-Meyer Assessment for the lower extremity (FMA-LE) was used to evaluate motor function. The scale comprises 17 items, grouped into 7 major categories, with a total possible score of 34 points. Each item is scored on a 3-point ordinal scale (0 = cannot perform; 1 = performs partially; 2 = performs fully). Higher total scores indicate better lower limb motor function. This scale is widely validated and commonly used to assess motor recovery in hemiplegic patients [23].

sec.title: Berg Balance Scale (BBS)
sec.p: Static and dynamic balance was assessed using the Berg Balance Scale (BBS). The scale consists of 14 functional tasks, each rated on a 5-point ordinal scale from 0 (cannot perform) to 4 (performs independently and safely). The total score ranges from 0 to 56, with higher scores indicating better balance ability [24].

sec.title: Modified Barthel Index (MBI)
sec.p: Functional independence in activities of daily living (ADLs) was measured using the Modified Barthel Index (MBI). This study focused on the lower-limb-related ADL items, including walking, transferring, stair climbing, and toileting, which together yield a sub-score ranging from 0 to 55 points. A higher score denotes a greater level of functional independence [25].

sec.title: 6-Minute Walk Test (6MWT)
sec.p: Walking endurance and functional capacity were assessed with the 6-Minute Walk Test (6MWT). Participants were instructed to walk back and forth along a marked, 30-meter corridor at their own pace for six minutes. The primary outcome was the total distance walked in meters [26].

sec.title: Manual Muscle Testing (MMT)
sec.p: The strength of key lower limb muscles—the iliopsoas, quadriceps, hamstrings, and tibialis anterior—was evaluated using Manual Muscle Testing (MMT). Strength was graded according to the standard 0–5 Medical Research Council (MRC) scale, where 0 represents no muscle contraction and 5 represents normal strength against full resistance. Testing was performed by an experienced assessor using standardized positioning to minimize compensatory movements [27].

sec.title: Statistical analysis
sec.p: Statistical analyses were performed using SPSS (Version 25.0). Continuous data are presented as mean ± standard deviation if normally distributed; otherwise, median (interquartile range) is reported. Categorical data are presented as frequencies and percentages, with between-group comparisons made using the Chi-square test or Fisher’s exact test, as appropriate. The primary analysis for between-group differences in post-intervention outcomes was conducted using Analysis of Covariance (ANCOVA), with the baseline score as a covariate. For secondary within-group comparisons across time (pre- vs. post-intervention), repeated-measures ANOVA was employed. Normality and homogeneity of variances were assessed using the Shapiro-Wilk and Levene’s tests, respectively. Effect sizes are reported as partial eta-squared (ηp2) for ANCOVA. The significance level was set at P < 0.05 (two-tailed). (* < 0.05, ** < 0.01, *** < 0.001).

sec.title: Infection control measures during the COVID-19 pandemic
sec.p: Conducted between July 2020 and December 2022, this study adhered to strict hospital infection control protocols during the COVID-19 pandemic. Safety measures included pre-session screening, universal masking, physical distancing, equipment sanitization, and provision of hand sanitizer. No sessions were interrupted due to participant COVID-19 infection.

sec.title: Combined RR and BFRT leads to greater improvement in lower limb motor function by the lower extremity functional movement assessment (LE-FMA)
sec.p:
At baseline, lower limb motor function as assessed by the FMA-LE did not differ significantly between the two groups (P = 0.384). Following the 8-week intervention, both groups demonstrated significant within-group improvements (both P < 0.001). However, the post-intervention FMA-LE score was significantly higher in the RR + BFRT group compared to the RR group (16.60 ± 4.61 vs. 14.53 ± 3.47, P = 0.026), indicating a superior treatment effect with the addition of BFRT (Table 3). Table 3
Comparison of lower limb motor function assessment (LE-FMA) between the two groups of patients pre- and post-rehabilitation therapy
GroupsPre-physical therapyPost-physical therapyt/x2P
RR8.25 ± 1.8214.53 ± 3.4712.037 < 0.001
RR + BFRT8.70 ± 2.7016.60 ± 4.6112.495 < 0.001
t/x20.8762.275
P0.3840.026

sec.title: BFRT provides additional benefits for balance recovery based on Berg Balance Scale (BBS) assessment
sec.p:
Balance function, measured by the BBS, also showed significant improvement in both groups after the intervention (both P < 0.001). The RR + BFRT group achieved a greater final BBS score than the RR group (44.08 ± 8.01 vs. 40.90 ± 5.07), and this between-group difference was statistically significant (P = 0.037) (Table 4). Table 4
Comparison of balance function (BBS) between the two groups of patients pre- and post-rehabilitation therapy
GroupsPre-physical therapyPost-physical therapyt/x2P
RR27.85 ± 3.4240.90 ± 5.0713.067 < 0.001
RR + BFRT26.08 ± 5.1244.08 ± 8.0116.529 < 0.001
t/x21.8222.118
P0.0720.037

sec.title: Superior efficacy of combined RR and BFRT on functional independence as measured by the Modified Barthel Index(MBI)
sec.p:
Functional independence related to lower-limb activities, assessed by the MBI, improved markedly in both groups (both P < 0.001). The degree of improvement was significantly greater in the RR + BFRT group, whose post-intervention MBI score was higher than that of the RR group (61.18 ± 11.46 vs. 53.13 ± 11.31, P = 0.002) (Table 5). Table 5
Comparison of activities of daily living (MBI) between the two groups of patients pre- and post-rehabilitation therapy
GroupsPre-physical therapyPost-physical therapyt/x2P
RR28.08 ± 5.9553.13 ± 11.3113.697 < 0.001
RR + BFRT29.73 ± 4.6061.18 ± 11.4616.756 < 0.001
t/x21.3873.163
P0.1690.002

sec.title: Combined RR and BFRT significantly enhances walking distance by the 6-min walk test (6-MWT)
sec.p:
Walking capacity, evaluated by the 6MWT, increased significantly from baseline in both groups (both P < 0.001). The RR + BFRT group walked a substantially longer distance post-intervention than the RR group (37.85 ± 8.72 m vs. 32.23 ± 6.14 m, P = 0.001) (Table 6). Table 6
Comparison of the 6-Minute Walk Test between the two groups of patients pre- and post-rehabilitation therapy
GroupsPrep-physical therapyPost-physical therapyt/x2P
RR12.23 ± 4.4032.23 ± 6.1428.730 < 0.001
RR + BFRT12.53 ± 4.1937.85 ± 8.7222.635 < 0.001
t/x20.3123.336
P0.7560.001

sec.title: BFRT enhances strength recovery in proximal muscles: a difference in manual muscle testing (MMT) outcomes
sec.p:
Manual muscle testing (MMT), a widely adopted assessment tool in rehabilitation medicine, was used to evaluate muscle strength. At baseline, MMT scores for the iliopsoas, quadriceps, hamstrings, and tibialis anterior muscles showed no significant differences between the two groups (all P > 0.05).Following the 8-week intervention, both groups exhibited significant within-group improvements in strength for all four muscle groups (all P < 0.01). More importantly, between-group comparisons of post-intervention scores revealed that the RR + BFRT group demonstrated significantly greater strength gains than the RR group in the iliopsoas (3.60 vs. 3.25, P = 0.022), quadriceps (3.20 vs. 2.85, P = 0.027), and hamstrings (3.25 vs. 2.85, P = 0.013). In contrast, no statistically significant between-group difference was observed in the strength of the tibialis anterior muscle ((RR + BFRT: 1.93 vs. RR: 1.88, P = 0.774)) (Table 7). Table 7
Comparison of the MMTs of the iliopsoas, quadriceps, hamstring, and tibialis anterior muscles between the two groups of patientspre- and post-rehabilitation therapy
GroupsRRRR + BFRTRR + BFRTP1P2P3P4
Prep-physical therapyPost-physical therapyPrep-physical therapyPost-physical therapy
Iliopsoas2.45 ± 0.503.25 ± 0.712.33 ± 0.623.60 ± 0.630.0000.0000.3230.022
Quadriceps2.33 ± 0.472.85 ± 0.702.18 ± 0.553.20 ± 0.690.0000.0000.1950.027
Hamstring2.25 ± 0.592.85 ± 0.742.10 ± 0.593.25 ± 0.670.0000.0000.2590.013
Tibialis anterior1.05 ± 0.501.88 ± 0.721.03 ± 0.861.93 ± 0.830.0000.0000.8750.774
P1, comparison of the RR group pre- and post-physical therapy; P2, comparison of the RR + BFRT group pre- and post-physical therapy; P3, comparison of RR and RR + BFRT pre-physical therapy; P4, comparison of RR and RR + BFRT post-physical therapy

sec.title: Discussion
sec.p: Stroke is a disease with high incidence, mortality, and disability rates, increasingly affecting younger individuals and imposing substantial social, familial, and economic burdens worldwide. More than 50% of stroke patients experience long-term chronic motor dysfunction. Unilateral limb weakness and abnormal muscle tone lead to atypical movement patterns, often manifesting as a characteristic “hemiplegic gait.” Clinically, only about 37% of patients regain independent walking, while 30%–40% continue to have limited walking ability or lose ambulatory function even after rehabilitation [28–30]. Therefore, promoting the recovery of lower limb motor function post-stroke is crucial. The ultimate goal of rehabilitation is to improve functional impairments, enhance self-care ability, and increase quality of life and social participation. Balance improvement and normalized gait patterns are major targets in post-stroke rehabilitation as they significantly increase independence, quality of life, and reduce fall risk.

BFRTinvolves the mechanical compression of blood vessels using an external device (e.g., a tourniquet) to reduce blood flow to the muscle, promoting blood pooling in the distal capillaries. Combining low-intensity resistance training with BFRT can mitigate risks associated with high-intensity training while achieving similar effects in increasing muscle strength, endurance, and hypertrophy [31]. Studies have shown that BFRT accelerates rehabilitation and reduces muscle atrophy after knee surgeries (e.g., anterior cruciate ligament repair, meniscus injury) [32]. BFRT alone can also alleviate disuse muscle atrophy under non-weight-bearing conditions, reduce pain, improve joint symptoms and limb function, and enhance quality of life [33].

This study found that after 8 weeks, the RR + BFRT group showed significantly greater improvements in lower extremity motor function (FMA-LE), balance (BBS), activities of daily living (MBI), and walking distance (6MWT) compared to the RR group alone. This indicates that BFRT combined with routine rehabilitation accelerates lower limb functional recovery more effectively than routine rehabilitation alone. The increased muscle strength likely contributes to better balance maintenance by training both agonist and antagonist muscles, thereby improving intermuscular coordination, which is essential for balance.

Beyond statistical significance, the clinical relevance and real-world impact of adding BFRT to rehabilitation are paramount. The observed improvements in the RR + BFRT group not only were statistically significant but also consistently exceeded established Minimal Clinically Important Difference (MCID) thresholds, indicating changes perceptible to patients. Specifically, the improvement in FMA-LE scores (7.9–10.8 points, exceeding the 5–6 point MCID) is associated with better motor control that may translate into safer mobility and reduced fall risk during daily activities. The gain in 6MWT distance (25.3–30.6 m, surpassing the 20–30 m MCID range) suggests an enhanced capacity for community ambulation, potentially enabling patients to walk to local destinations or participate in family activities with greater independence and less fatigue. Most pivotally, the substantial increase in MBI scores (31.5–36.5 points) directly reflects greater functional independence, particularly in lower-limb-dependent tasks like transfers and toileting, which can reduce caregiver burden and profoundly improve a patient’s dignity and self-efficacy. Therefore, the addition of BFRT appears to provide a disproportionate boost in functional capacity and independence—core determinants of quality of life post-stroke—and may accelerate the recovery trajectory toward practical, life-enhancing autonomy. Furthermore, combined BFRT training demonstrated significantly greater improvement in the major proximal muscle groups (iliopsoas, quadriceps, hamstrings) compared to conventional rehabilitation alone. However, no significant intergroup difference was observed in the strength recovery of the tibialis anterior muscle. A potential mechanism for this finding may lie in the primary action of BFRT, which promotes the recruitment and hypertrophy of type II muscle fibers largely through metabolic stress [34]. This mechanism is particularly effective for powerful, anti-gravity proximal muscle groups responsible for weight-bearing and propulsion [35]. In contrast, the functional recovery of the tibialis anterior—the primary dorsiflexor—relies more heavily on the remodeling of central neural pathways and fine motor control. Post-stroke, the reinnervation and recovery of distal muscles often lag behind that of proximal muscles [36, 37]. Consequently, although BFRT provides a potent stimulus for muscle hypertrophy, its efficacy in enhancing strength may be attenuated in muscles like the tibialis anterior, where recovery is more constrained by neural limitations. Future studies might explore combining BFRT with more targeted interventions for ankle dorsiflexion, such as neuromuscular electrical stimulation or robot-assisted training. The findings are consistent with previous research in orthopedic and sports rehabilitation, where BFRT has been shown to enhance strength and functional performance in proximal lower limb muscles [15, 38, 39]. This study extends these observations to neurorehabilitation, systematically revealing that the effects of BFRT on muscular recovery may be selective.

Some reports indicate that BFRT may cause adverse effects such as limb numbness, delayed muscle pain, or potentially increase cardiovascular and cerebrovascular risks [40]. In this study, with the exception of one fatality due to a non-treatment-related heart failure complication, no other serious adverse events were reported in either group. Overall, BFRT conducted under professional guidance and supervision is widely regarded as a safe and effective training modality.

sec.title: Limitations and perspectives
sec.p: This study has several limitations that should be considered when interpreting the findings. First, we employed a fixed BFR pressure (160–170 mmHg) rather than pressures individualized to each participant’s arterial occlusion pressure (AOP), which may affect the uniformity of the ischemic stimulus. Second, the sample size, while adequate for detecting the primary effect, was moderate and recruited from a single center, which may limit the generalizability of the results. Third, the lack of long-term follow-up assessment means we cannot comment on the persistence of the observed benefits. Fourth, the multi-modal nature of the conventional rehabilitation program makes it difficult to isolate the specific synergistic effect of BFRT from other components. Fifth, while routine vital signs were monitored and no related adverse events were reported, we did not conduct systematic cardiovascular assessments (e.g., continuous hemodynamic monitoring, cardiac ultrasound) to evaluate the acute or chronic cardiovascular responses to BFRT in this stroke population. Finally, the differential response of the tibialis anterior muscle suggests that our intervention may not have been optimal for training this specific muscle group. Future multi-center trials with individualized BFR pressures, longer follow-up, and more targeted exercise regimens are warranted to confirm and extend our findings.

sec.title: Conclusion
sec.p: BFRTas an adjunct to routine rehabilitation, effectively enhances lower limb motor function, balance, walking capacity, and proximal muscle strength in stroke patients with hemiplegia, leading to greater functional independence. The intervention was safe and well-tolerated. BFRT represents a promising and practical adjunctive therapy worthy of further clinical application and research in stroke rehabilitation.

fn.p: Springer Nature remains neutral with regard to jurisdictional claims in published maps and institutional affiliations.

sec.title: Publisher’s note
sec.p: All claims expressed in this article are solely those of the authors and do not necessarily represent those of their affiliated organizations or those of the publisher, the editors, and the reviewers. Any product that may be evaluated in this article or claim that may be made by its manufacturer is not guaranteed or endorsed by the publisher.

notes.title: Authors’ contributions
notes.p: Z.Q conceived and designed the study. Z.Q and W.Q carried out the experiments. Z.Q and L.B contributed to the assessment tool and data analysis. Z.Q, L.B, W.Q, W.Li, J.J, and Lin Xia contributed to the interpretation of the results. Z.Q took the lead in writing the manuscript. Z.Q, L.B, and Lin Xia provided critical feedback and helped shape the research, analysis, and manuscript. All authors read and approved the manuscript.

notes.title: Funding
notes.p: This work was supported by the Scientific Research Project of Science and Technology Department of Sichuan Province (Sichuan Natural Science Foundation 2023NSFSC1619) and Research and Development Plan Project of Affiliated Hospital of North Sichuan Medical College (2020JC023).

notes.title: Data availability
notes.p: The original contributions presented in the study are included in the article; further inquiries can be directed to the corresponding authors. The datasets generated and/or analysed during the current study are available in the [https://figshare.com/] repository, [https://figshare.com/account/items/27867969/edit].

notes.title: Ethics approval and consent to participate
notes.p: The studies involving human participants were reviewed and approved by the Ethics Committee of the Affiliated Hospital of North Sichuan Medical College (Number:2023ER378-1). Chinese Clinical Research Registration Center (ChiCTR2500095271). The patients/participants provided written informed consent to participate in this study. Written informed consent was obtained from the individual(s) for the publication of any potentially identifiable images or data included in this article.

notes.title: Competing interests
notes.p: The authors declare that they have no competing interests.
